result:
There is a peculiar kind of magic at the edge of the ocean, where the land hesitates before surrendering to the sea. I love the tide pools—the shallow, rocky cups left behind by the retreating waves. They are miniature worlds, entire ecosystems contained within a few inches of cold, saltwater. If you crouch down and squint, a tide pool becomes a galaxy. The ochre sea stars are slow-moving planets, creeping along the granite. Anemones are nebulae, their translucent tentacles reaching blindly upward, waiting for the universe to drop a morsel of food into their grasp. Tiny hermit crabs scuttle like comets, searching for a newer, larger shell to call home.

What fascinates me most is the resilience of these micro-worlds. Twice a day, the ocean reclaims them, crashing in with a cold, violent embrace, stirring the sand, and resetting the scene. The air tastes of salt and ruin, a pungent reminder that creation and destruction are neighbors. Then, the water retreats, leaving them exposed to the harsh sun and greedy gulls. Yet, they endure. They close up, hold onto their moisture, and wait. 

We are not so different from these tide pools, I think. We hold our own universes within us—our private ecosystems of thought, memory, and feeling. The tides of life crash over us, threatening to wash away our boundaries. But when the water recedes, we remain, clinging to the rock, waiting for the next wave.
